headlineSummary: HN sentiment skewed negative overall, driven by privacy, latency, and trust complaints about OpenAI and Anthropic, while Microsoft Copilot and Google Gemini were only lightly discussed and close to each other [E1][E3][E2][E7].

ranking.summary: OpenAI drew a negative tone from a privacy/usability complaint about ChatGPT and another report of heavy lag, partly offset by one positive mention of Codex helping an iterative workflow [E1][E2][E5].

Anthropic was also negative overall, with a strong trust criticism of Claude Code plus a complaint about verbose output, though there were also positive remarks about Claude/Claude Code helping produce reliable results [E3][E4][E5][E6].

Microsoft Copilot landed around neutral with limited evidence, as one comment associated it with the same corporate-firewall lag complaint while another praised it as making code more reliable and maintainable [E2][E6].
Google Gemini was mildly positive on limited evidence, with a single comment listing Gemini among helpful ways to learn non-fiction [E7].

evidence.excerpt: ChatGPT won't let you type until Cloudflare reads your React state

annotations.rationale: The title explicitly names ChatGPT and frames OpenAI's product as blocking user input until Cloudflare inspects client-side state, a strongly critical privacy/usability claim.

evidence.excerpt: Ah, this explains chatgpt (and probably copilot) performance behind corporate firewalls such as zscaler. Between the network latency and low end machines, there is an enormous lag between chatgpts response and being able to reply, especially for editing a canvas. I've been sitting there for up to a minute plus waiting to be able to use the canvas controls or highlight text after an update.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly names chatgpt and complains about enormous lag and minute-plus waits, expressing a clear negative usability/performance judgment.
The commenter says the same issue probably affects copilot, implying negative performance/usability sentiment, though the exact Copilot product is less certain.

evidence.excerpt: Claude Code runs Git reset –hard origin/main against project repo every 10 mins

annotations.rationale: The title explicitly names Claude Code and accuses it of repeatedly running a destructive git command against a project repo, which is a strong trust/safety criticism.

evidence.excerpt: I asked Claude to convert lazy config to pack and I was not happy with it because how verbose it turned out

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly says Claude produced an unsatisfactory, overly verbose result, a direct negative assessment of output quality.

evidence.excerpt: This was achieved through showing Claude Code and Codex the browsers ground truth, and have them measure & iterate against those at every significant container width, running over weeks

annotations.rationale: The excerpt credits Claude Code with helping achieve an impressive technical result through iterative measurement work, indicating positive usefulness/capability.
The excerpt similarly credits Codex with contributing to the successful result, implying positive usefulness/capability.

evidence.excerpt: my repos have commits authored by Claude, Dependabot, GitHub Advanced Security Bot, Copilot, etc. All of this makes the code more reliable and maintainable, for both human and AI authored code.

annotations.rationale: The commenter includes Claude among tools whose contributions make code more reliable and maintainable, a direct positive judgment.
The commenter includes Copilot among tools making code more reliable and maintainable, expressing clear positive sentiment toward the tool's contribution.

evidence.excerpt: We now have other ways to learn non-fiction: Wikipedia, Veritasium, Gemini, etc.

annotations.rationale: Gemini is cited as one of the useful ways people can now learn non-fiction, which conveys mild positive usefulness sentiment.
